frameworks.name: NIST AI Risk Management Framework
frameworks.summary: The foundational U.S. federal framework for managing AI risk across the full system lifecycle. Organized around four core functions: GOVERN, MAP, MEASURE, MANAGE. Risk-based rather than prescriptive; it provides structure for identifying, assessing, and responding to AI risks without mandating specific controls. Widely adopted as a reference baseline by enterprise risk programs, federal agencies, and framework crosswalk authors.
frameworks.gaps: No NHI or machine identity controls. No behavioral or runtime authorization model. Agentic AI mentioned only in supplementary profiles, not in the core framework. Static risk posture assessment does not address post-deployment behavioral drift. Designed for general AI systems, not the multi-agent orchestration threat surface.

frameworks.name: NIST AI RMF Playbook
frameworks.summary: Companion implementation guide to the AI RMF. Provides suggested actions, references, and subcategories for each RMF function and category. Structured as a playbook with GOVERN, MAP, MEASURE, and MANAGE plays. Intended to help organizations operationalize the framework rather than just adopt it as policy.
frameworks.gaps: Implementation actions are framed for conventional AI systems. No NHI lifecycle management, no agent identity, no runtime intent classification. MEASURE play covers performance metrics but not behavioral authorization or declared-vs-operational intent divergence. Lacks agentic-specific threat scenarios.

frameworks.name: NIST AI 600-1 GenAI Profile
frameworks.summary: GenAI-specific profile of the AI RMF published July 2024. Identifies 12 generative AI risk categories including CBRN information, data privacy, confabulation, homogenization, human-AI configuration, information integrity, IP, obscene content, system prompt leakage, and societal harms. Maps each risk category to the four RMF functions. Companion to but distinct from AI 100-2 (adversarial ML taxonomy).
frameworks.gaps: GenAI risks are modeled as content and output risks. No treatment of agentic deployment patterns, tool-calling architectures, or multi-agent orchestration. NHI not addressed. Behavioral authorization and runtime intent classification are absent. Focuses on LLM inference-time risk, not agent runtime governance.

frameworks.name: NIST AI 800-4 Managing Misuse Risk
frameworks.summary: Initial public draft published March 2025. Addresses the risk of AI systems being used for harmful purposes beyond their intended scope. Covers dual-use AI capabilities, misuse threat modeling, and organizational practices for limiting harmful downstream use. Relevant to agentic AI in that autonomous agents expand attack surface for misuse scenarios, though this is not the primary framing.
frameworks.gaps: Misuse framing focuses on intentional human-directed abuse, not emergent agent behavior. No behavioral authorization controls. NHI not addressed. Agentic misuse scenarios (an agent taking harmful autonomous action) not explicitly modeled. Still in initial public draft as of session date.

frameworks.name: ISO/IEC 42001:2023 AI Management System
frameworks.summary: First international AI management system standard. Mirrors ISO 27001 structure: organizational context, leadership, planning, support, operation, performance evaluation, and improvement. Provides a certifiable framework for AI governance programs. Increasingly referenced alongside EU AI Act compliance roadmaps. Annex A includes 38 AI controls covering development, deployment, and decommissioning.
frameworks.gaps: Management system framework: strong on governance structure, weak on technical controls. No NHI controls, no agent identity lifecycle, no behavioral authorization. Agentic AI is not addressed as a distinct deployment archetype. Control Annex A is high-level and requires significant interpretation for operational implementation.

frameworks.name: EU AI Act
frameworks.summary: Regulation (EU) 2024/1689. Entered into force August 1, 2024. Phased enforcement: prohibited practices and AI literacy (Art. 4+5) from February 2, 2025; GPAI obligations from August 2, 2025; high-risk system requirements from August 2, 2026. Risk-tiered: unacceptable risk (prohibited), high-risk (Annex III), limited risk, minimal risk. GPAI models above 10^25 FLOPs face additional systemic risk requirements.
frameworks.gaps: Regulation is framed around AI providers, deployers, and importers. No treatment of NHI or machine credential governance. Agentic AI mentioned in recitals but not operationalized in technical control requirements. Behavioral authorization and runtime intent not addressed. High-risk classification is use-case based, not deployment-architecture based.

frameworks.name: OECD AI Principles
frameworks.summary: Adopted May 2019, updated May 2024 to address generative AI and frontier models. Five principles: inclusive growth and well-being; human-centered values and fairness; transparency and explainability; robustness, security, and safety; accountability. Foundational policy reference adopted by G7, G20, and over 40 countries. Informs rather than mandates; no technical controls specified.
frameworks.gaps: Principles-based framework with no technical control specification. NHI, behavioral authorization, and agentic AI architecture are completely unaddressed. Influential as policy scaffolding but insufficient for operational governance of deployed AI systems.

frameworks.name: UNESCO AI Ethics Recommendation
frameworks.summary: First global normative framework on AI ethics adopted by all 193 UNESCO member states. Covers the full AI lifecycle with emphasis on human rights, dignity, sustainability, privacy, non-discrimination, and transparency. Includes Ethical Impact Assessment methodology. Referenced by the EU AI Act recitals and OECD AI principles.
frameworks.gaps: Normative and aspirational. Zero technical control mapping. No treatment of NHI, agentic AI, or behavioral authorization. Valuable as an ethical foundation but requires substantial translation before operational implementation.

frameworks.name: OWASP LLM Top 10
frameworks.summary: OWASP's ranked list of the ten most critical security risks for LLM applications. 2025 edition covers: LLM01 Prompt Injection, LLM02 Sensitive Information Disclosure, LLM03 Supply Chain, LLM04 Data and Model Poisoning, LLM05 Improper Output Handling, LLM06 Excessive Agency, LLM07 System Prompt Leakage, LLM08 Vector and Embedding Weaknesses, LLM09 Misinformation, LLM10 Unbounded Consumption. LLM06 (Excessive Agency) directly addresses over-permissioned agents.
frameworks.gaps: LLM06 partially addresses agentic risk but frames it as a permission-scoping problem, not a behavioral authorization problem. No NHI identity lifecycle or machine credential management. Runtime intent classification not modeled. Mapping to identity controls is shallow. Best suited for application-layer LLM risk, not full agentic system governance.

frameworks.name: OWASP Agentic AI Security Top 10
frameworks.summary: Published 2026, the first OWASP list specifically targeting agentic AI threat patterns. Covers: ASI01 Memory Poisoning, ASI02 Tool Misuse, ASI03 Privilege Escalation, ASI04 Resource Overuse, ASI05 AI Supply Chain, ASI06 Data Exfiltration, ASI07 Agent Communication Attacks, ASI08 Authorization Bypass, ASI09 Prompt Injection in Agent Context, ASI10 Rogue Agents. Includes Appendix C mapping to OWASP NHI Top 10 and Appendix D with AIVSS scoring.
frameworks.gaps: NHI coverage partial: agent identity addressed in ASI03 and ASI08, but no full NHI lifecycle. Behavioral authorization is implicitly addressed in ASI08 but without a runtime intent model. No DIRA-style declared-vs-operational intent classification. Crosswalk to identity standards is limited.

frameworks.name: OWASP AI Security Verification Standard
frameworks.summary: OWASP's verification standard for AI systems, modeled after ASVS (Application Security Verification Standard). Provides testable requirements organized by control category and maturity level. Covers model security, data pipeline security, inference security, deployment controls, and operational monitoring. Intended for use in security assessments and audits of AI applications.
frameworks.gaps: NHI controls are present but not comprehensive. Behavioral authorization testing not fully addressed. Agentic deployment patterns partially covered. Runtime intent verification is absent. The standard is maturing and some control areas are still being developed.

frameworks.name: NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5
frameworks.summary: The canonical U.S. federal security control catalog. AI-relevant families include: AC (Access Control), IA (Identification and Authentication), SI (System and Information Integrity), AU (Audit and Accountability), SC (System and Communications Protection). IA-9 covers service identification and authentication, the closest existing control to NHI. Widely used as the crosswalk anchor for mapping other frameworks.
frameworks.gaps: Written for conventional information systems. IA-9 provides a hook for service/machine identity but is underspecified for dynamic agent credentials and short-lived tokens. No controls for runtime behavioral authorization. Agentic AI threat patterns not addressed. AC family covers authorization but as static policy, not runtime intent classification.

frameworks.name: NIST SP 800-218A Secure Software Development for AI
frameworks.summary: Supplement to NIST SP 800-218 (SSDF) that extends secure software development practices to AI and ML systems. Published May 2024. Covers data security for training pipelines, model development security, AI-specific testing requirements, and supply chain risks in ML toolchains. Intended for AI developers and organizations integrating AI into software products.
frameworks.gaps: Development and supply chain focused. No treatment of runtime governance, NHI, behavioral authorization, or post-deployment monitoring. Agentic AI architectures not addressed. Useful as a development-time complement but does not cover the deployment-time threat surface where behavioral authorization matters most.

frameworks.name: CISA/NSA AI Security Joint Guidance
frameworks.summary: Joint guidance issued November 2024 by CISA, NSA, FBI, and international partners (ASD ACSC, BSI, NCSC-UK, NCSC-NL, NCSC-NZ). Addresses secure deployment of AI systems in operational environments with particular attention to critical infrastructure. Covers supply chain risk, access control, model integrity monitoring, and incident response for AI deployments.
frameworks.gaps: NHI coverage is partial: service account access controls mentioned but machine identity lifecycle not addressed. No behavioral authorization or runtime intent model. Agentic AI deployment patterns referenced but not operationalized. The guidance is prescriptive for conventional deployments and does not address the novel attack surface of autonomous agent systems.

frameworks.name: MITRE ATLAS
frameworks.summary: MITRE's Adversarial Threat Landscape for AI Systems. Knowledge base of adversary tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs) targeting AI components. Structured analogously to MITRE ATT&CK. Covers reconnaissance, resource development, initial access, ML model attack, defense evasion, and impact. Includes case studies from real-world AI system attacks. v4.9.0 released October 2024.
frameworks.gaps: NHI not addressed. Behavioral authorization not modeled as a defensive control. Agentic AI patterns are emerging in supplementary materials but not fully integrated into the main matrix in v4.9.0. Focus is on attacker TTPs, not governance or authorization frameworks.

frameworks.name: MITRE ATLAS Agentic AI Supplement
frameworks.summary: Supplementary material extending ATLAS to cover adversary TTPs specific to agentic AI deployments. Addresses tool misuse, inter-agent manipulation, orchestrator compromise, persistent agent memory attacks, and privilege escalation in multi-agent pipelines. Developed in response to the rapid expansion of autonomous AI deployments and the novel attack surface they create.
frameworks.gaps: TTP-focused: describes what attackers do, not what defenders should implement. NHI lifecycle and identity-centric controls not fully addressed. Behavioral authorization and runtime intent classification absent as defensive concepts. Best used alongside identity-focused frameworks, not as a standalone governance tool.

frameworks.name: AIUC-1 Agentic AI Underwriting Criteria
frameworks.summary: First certifiable standard for agentic AI systems. Published by the AIUC-1 Consortium (Stanford Trustworthy AI Research, DelphinusCyber, FormerGov.com, and enterprise CISOs). Establishes underwriting criteria for assessing risk and insurability of autonomous AI agents. Covers principal hierarchy, agent identity lifecycle, permission scoping, behavioral attestation, and incident response. Aligned with VerifiedAgents.ai certification program (243 granular control objectives).
frameworks.gaps: Emerging standard: adoption is nascent and the certification infrastructure is still being built out. Behavioral authorization model addresses scoping and attestation but does not specify runtime intent classification at the depth of DIRA. NHI coverage is strong for agent identity but lighter on machine credential management outside the agent context.

frameworks.name: Singapore Model AI Governance Framework for Agentic AI
frameworks.summary: Published January 2026 by IMDA (Singapore's Info-communications Media Development Authority). The most operationally mature national governance framework for agentic AI. Built on four pillars: (1) Principal Hierarchy: explicit chain of accountability from human principal through orchestrator to sub-agents; (2) Scoped Permissions: least-privilege, bounded capability grants per task; (3) Minimal Footprint: agents acquire only the data and access required for the immediate task; (4) Auditable Decisions: all agent actions must be logged and attributable. Directly addresses the governance gap that NIST AI RMF and ISO 42001 leave open.
frameworks.gaps: Strong governance framework but lacks binding enforcement mechanisms outside Singapore's regulatory context. Behavioral authorization is addressed through scoped permissions and auditability but does not specify a runtime intent classification protocol. NHI coverage is strong at the principal hierarchy layer but does not prescribe specific credential formats or lifecycle protocols.

frameworks.name: NIST IR 8587 Non-Human Entity Identity Management
frameworks.summary: NIST initial public draft addressing identity management for non-human entities: service accounts, API clients, machine identities, bots, and AI agents. Establishes lifecycle framework: registration, provisioning, authentication, authorization, and decommissioning. Addresses credential management, short-lived token patterns, and workload identity. The first NIST publication to treat NHI as a first-class identity management problem.
frameworks.gaps: Still in initial public draft; not yet a finalized standard. Behavioral authorization is out of scope; the publication covers identity and credential management, not runtime intent classification. Agentic AI mentioned but not the primary focus. Does not specify runtime authorization models or declared-vs-operational intent divergence detection.

frameworks.name: OWASP Non-Human Identities Top 10
frameworks.summary: Published 2025. Ten ranked risks for non-human identity management: NHI1 Improper Offboarding, NHI2 Secret Leakage, NHI3 Vulnerable Third-Party NHIs, NHI4 Insecure Authentication, NHI5 Over-Privileged NHIs, NHI6 Insecure Cloud Deployment Configurations, NHI7 Long-Lived Secrets, NHI8 Environment Isolation Failure, NHI9 NHI Reuse, NHI10 Human Access to NHI Secrets. Covers service accounts, API keys, certificates, and agent credentials. Directly maps to OWASP Agentic AI Top 10 Appendix C.
frameworks.gaps: Identity and credential lifecycle focused. Behavioral authorization is outside scope. Agentic AI only addressed in Appendix C crosswalk. Runtime intent classification, declared-vs-operational intent divergence, and behavioral drift detection are not covered. Best used alongside AIUC-1 and Singapore MGF for full agentic identity governance.

frameworks.name: SPIFFE/SPIRE Workload Identity
frameworks.summary: CNCF project providing cryptographically verifiable workload identity for cloud-native systems. SPIFFE (Secure Production Identity Framework for Everyone) defines the identity standard via SVIDs (SPIFFE Verifiable Identity Documents) in X.509 or JWT format. SPIRE is the reference implementation. Issues short-lived, attestation-based credentials to workloads without long-lived secrets. The most mature open standard for solving machine-to-machine authentication in zero-trust architectures.
frameworks.gaps: Solves the identity issuance and attestation problem but not the authorization problem. SPIFFE establishes who the workload is; it does not govern what the workload is permitted to do based on declared intent. No behavioral authorization layer. Agentic AI deployment patterns (multi-agent, MCP servers, tool-calling) are not addressed in current specifications.

frameworks.name: EU AI Act High-Risk Annex III
frameworks.summary: Annex III of the EU AI Act enumerating high-risk AI use cases subject to conformity assessment, technical documentation, and post-market monitoring obligations. Eight high-risk categories: critical infrastructure, education, employment, access to essential services, law enforcement, migration and border control, administration of justice, biometric identification. High-risk systems must register in the EU database and undergo conformity assessment before deployment.
frameworks.gaps: Use-case classification approach: Annex III lists risk categories but does not prescribe technical controls for AI system security. NHI not addressed. Behavioral authorization not specified. Agentic AI deployment as a distinct architectural pattern not addressed in Annex III; an autonomous agent serving a high-risk function falls under the use-case category, not a separate archetype.

frameworks.name: NIST AI 100-2 Adversarial ML Taxonomy
frameworks.summary: Comprehensive taxonomy of adversarial machine learning attacks and mitigations, finalized January 2024. Covers: evasion attacks, poisoning attacks, privacy attacks (extraction, inversion, membership inference), and abuse attacks. Includes GenAI-specific attacks: prompt injection, jailbreaking, training data extraction, and model extraction. Widely used as the shared vocabulary for AI security threat discussions.
frameworks.gaps: Attack taxonomy: authoritative on threat classification but not a control framework. NHI, behavioral authorization, and agentic AI architectures are not the focus. Published before the agentic AI deployment pattern became dominant. Useful as a threat vocabulary for crosswalk mapping but does not address runtime governance.

frameworks.name: CSA Cloud Controls Matrix v4
frameworks.summary: CSA Cloud Controls Matrix v4.0.12, updated June 2024. 197 controls across 17 security domains including Application & Interface Security (AIS), Identity & Access Management (IAM), Infrastructure & Virtualization Security (IVS), and Supply Chain Management (STA). Cross-mapped to NIST 800-53r5, ISO 27001:2022, and CIS Controls v8. The standard cloud provider assurance framework used in CAIQ assessments.
frameworks.gaps: Cloud infrastructure focused with limited AI-specific controls. AI governance controls are not a native domain. NHI coverage exists implicitly via IAM domain but machine identity lifecycle for AI agents is not addressed. Behavioral authorization and agentic AI threat patterns are outside scope. Requires overlay (see NIST AI Overlays concept paper) to apply to AI deployments.

frameworks.name: CISA SBOM for AI
frameworks.summary: CISA guidance published May 2026 extending Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) requirements to AI systems. Defines an AI-BOM framework covering model provenance, training data lineage, dependency graphs for ML toolchains, and integration with CycloneDX AI extensions. Aligns with OWASP AIBOM work and NTIA SBOM minimum elements. Addresses AI supply chain transparency for federal and critical infrastructure operators.
frameworks.gaps: Supply chain transparency focused. No NHI controls. No behavioral authorization. Agentic AI partially addressed through agent dependency tracking but runtime governance is outside scope. The AI-BOM establishes what components are present, not how they behave at runtime or how agent credentials are managed.

frameworks.name: Dual-Intent Runtime Authorization
frameworks.summary: Runtime authorization framework for agentic AI systems. DIRA classifies agent intent at invocation time, not at provisioning time. The core insight: static RBAC and ABAC answer 'what is this identity permitted to do?' DIRA answers 'what is this agent actually attempting right now, and does that match what it declared?' Distinguishes declared intent (the stated purpose of the agent action, embedded in the request) from operational intent (the actual resource access pattern and capability reach being exercised). Divergence between declared and operational intent is a primary signal for behavioral drift, privilege escalation, and prompt injection under agentic execution. DIRA provides the authorization layer that NHI frameworks (NIST IR 8587, OWASP NHI) and identity standards (SPIFFE/SPIRE) leave open: they establish who the agent is, DIRA governs what it is permitted to do given what it claims to be doing.
frameworks.gaps: Active research framework. Reference implementation in progress. Not yet a ratified standard. Designed to fill the gap between existing NHI/identity frameworks and the runtime governance needs of autonomous agentic systems.
